Curriculum → Low-level design interview prep → Object modeling and domain relationships → Workflow orchestration and state modeling

Lesson 3 of 3

Workflow orchestration and state modeling

Separate durable state from coordination logic so long-running workflows remain understandable and extensible.

25-35 min LLD flow Object modeling and domain relationships 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

LLD prompts often expand from a single method to a multi-step workflow. Good designs keep orchestration explicit instead of burying it across many collaborating objects.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Model the command flow Choose one clear entry point for each user action2. Keep domain state authoritative Let domain objects decide whether a transition is legal3. Prepare for follow-up requirements Leave room for side-effect handlers or observers

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing LLD prompts often expand from a single method to a multi-step workflow. Good designs keep orchestration explicit instead of burying it across many collaborating objects. 2. Model the command flow Choose one clear entry point for each user action3. Keep domain state authoritative Let domain objects decide whether a transition is legal4. Prepare for follow-up requirements Leave room for side-effect handlers or observers
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

LLD prompts often expand from a single method to a multi-step workflow. Good designs keep orchestration explicit instead of burying it across many collaborating objects.

What to say first

  • Identify the entry point for each major workflow.
  • Keep state transitions owned by domain objects.
  • Separate orchestration from persistence and side effects.
2

Study section

Model the command flow

Write down which object receives the command, which validations happen first, and how downstream collaborators are invoked.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Choose one clear entry point for each user action
  • Keep orchestration readable from top to bottom
  • Distinguish synchronous validation from eventual work
3

Study section

Keep domain state authoritative

Workflows should ask aggregates or entities to change state rather than bypassing them and mutating their data directly.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Let domain objects decide whether a transition is legal
  • Emit explicit events or callbacks when later actions depend on the change
  • Avoid duplicating status logic in services and entities
4

Study section

Prepare for follow-up requirements

Interview prompts commonly add retries, cancellations, notifications, or audit logs. Design the workflow so those steps can be attached without rewriting the core model.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Leave room for side-effect handlers or observers
  • Keep long-running concerns out of the entity itself
  • Show how new steps can be inserted without changing the main contract

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Identify the entry point for each major workflow.
  • Keep state transitions owned by domain objects.
  • Separate orchestration from persistence and side effects.
  • Explain how the workflow absorbs common follow-up requirements.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Hiding a long workflow behind a single vague service method.
  • Mutating entity internals from orchestration code.
  • Tying notifications or persistence so tightly to the workflow that every change ripples everywhere.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • How would you structure a booking or checkout workflow?
  • Where should orchestration stop and domain ownership begin?
  • How would you add cancellation or retries without breaking the model?

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later.

0/3 saved Step 1/3: Explain the concept

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Explain the concept

Teach "Workflow orchestration and state modeling" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Guardrails

  • How would you structure a booking or checkout workflow?
  • Model the command flow
  • Keep domain state authoritative
  • Identify the entry point for each major workflow.

Suggested Structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
